quote.text: Better is the end of a thing than the beginning thereof: and the patient in spirit is better than the proud in spirit.
quote.author: Ecclesiastes 7:8 (King James Version)

stories.headline: The sixty-day peace lasted twenty
stories.dek: Three tankers burned on Tuesday; eighty targets burned overnight; by Wednesday the President was calling the deal "over" from a NATO podium. The ceasefire signed on 17–18 June has collapsed in the very week Iran buried the leader whose death began the war.

stories.kicker: Singapore · Transport & the Cost of Moving
stories.headline: The loop closes on Sunday
stories.dek: Seventeen years after its first stations opened, the Circle Line finally becomes a circle: Keppel, Cantonment and Prince Edward Road open 12 July. In the same week, the right to drive a car to any of them cost more than it ever has.

stories.kicker: The Macro Desk · Four Instruments, One Shock
stories.headline: The week the market un-priced the peace
stories.dek: Monday: a record Dow. Tuesday: a chip rout. Wednesday: oil up five per cent, minutes showing a Fed split toward hiking, and 586 Dow points gone. The regime didn't change this week — it got stress-tested from two directions at once.

stories.headline: Turf City returns at three thousand a foot
stories.dek: The first private homes in the old Turf City in a generation preview tomorrow at close to S$3,000 psf — while Lentor sells the same week at S$2,350, and a record 188 HDB flats changed hands above a million dollars in June. The ladder is stretching at both ends.

stories.kicker: Technology · The AI Trade's Earnings Test
stories.headline: A nineteen-fold profit, and it wasn't enough
stories.dek: Samsung's operating profit multiplied nineteen times over — and the stock fell seven per cent, torching chip shares worldwide, two days before SK hynix prices the largest US listing a foreign company has ever attempted. "Priced for perfection" just met its first earnings season.

stories.kicker: The Kit · Value, With a Memory
stories.headline: The $99 earbuds that remember what you heard
stories.dek: Nothing's Ear (3a) lands at a hundred dollars with a party trick the flagships don't have: pinch, and the last sixty seconds of your life are saved. The reviews call it the best bargain in audio. The feature deserves a harder look than the price.

stories.kicker: The Connected Home · Presence, Not Pictures
stories.headline: The house that knows you're there — without watching
stories.dek: A small arms race broke out in millimetre-wave radar this month: SwitchBot launched a battery-powered presence sensor days after Aqara announced its own. For the migrator, presence detection is the upgrade that makes automations finally feel intelligent — and radar does it without a lens.

stories.kicker: The Good Life · The Collectors Return
stories.headline: The gavel came back first
stories.dek: Phillips' spring season closed with watch sales up sixty per cent — hard numbers saying the collector market has re-heated after two cooling years. And from Japan, Seiko refreshes the most charming gateway drug in watchmaking: the Cocktail Time.

stories.headline: The Emmys reward the long shift
stories.dek: Nominations landed Wednesday: The Pitt leads everything with 25, Hacks sets a comedy record with 24, and Vince Gilligan's Pluribus puts hard science fiction in the Best Drama race. Television's big tent, this year, belongs to people who simply kept working.

stories.kicker: The Family Desk · Sport & the Sideline
stories.headline: Who really ends a childhood sport
stories.dek: The average child quits organised sport just before thirteen — and in a large national survey published this spring, girls who walked away were roughly twice as likely as boys to name a parent's sideline behaviour among the reasons. The ending, in other words, is often adult-authored.

stories.headline: Thirty-one lamps from the dawn
stories.dek: Euclid — the telescope Europe built to weigh dark energy — has stumbled into a different record: thirty-one newly confirmed quasars from the Universe's first billion years, two of them the most ancient ever seen. The side quest is becoming the mission.

stories.kicker: Love & Life · The Stories We Tell About Endings
stories.headline: When she earns more, the old explanation fails
stories.dek: Couples where the woman out-earns her partner do separate more often — that pattern replicates. But a new study in the Journal of Marriage and Family finds the standard explanation, wounded traditional gender norms, doesn't hold up. The ending is real; the story we tell about it may be wrong.

stories.kicker: Fitness · The Molecular Case for Starting Now
stories.headline: The brake on ageing muscle — and the pedal that releases it
stories.dek: A Singapore-led study names a molecular reason muscle declines with age: a regulator called DEAF1 rises, jams the cell's self-cleaning, and stalls repair. Exercise, it turns out, is the switch that turns DEAF1 back down. It is a lab result, not a prescription — but it explains why "use it or lose it" is literally true.

stories.headline: What's worth booking
stories.dek: A national birthday, a Formula 1 exhibition making its Asian debut, teamLab reopening a signature zone in Tokyo — and, still, the concert this city has been waiting all summer to buy a ticket to.

stories.headline: The Sense of an Ending
stories.dek: This was a week of endings — one proclaimed from a podium, one closed by a train. Between the two lies the whole difference the ancients meant when they said the end of a thing is better than its beginning: that finishing is a discipline, not an announcement, and that the endings we declare are usually the ones that don't hold.
